lesson: For unweighted shortest-path BFS, track distance alongside each queued node as (node, dist) tuples and mark visited at enqueue time, not dequeue, to avoid duplicate re-enqueues.
task: BFS shortest path in adjacency-list graph

lesson: For grid flood-fill/island counting, mark cells visited at push time (not pop time) in the DFS stack to prevent the same cell being pushed multiple times by different neighbors.
task: Count islands in a 0/1 grid via iterative DFS

lesson: To enumerate connected components (not just count them), collect nodes into a per-start list during the DFS/BFS traversal itself instead of a separate pass, since visited-set membership already partitions nodes correctly.
task: List connected components of an undirected graph

lesson: Detect a cycle in an undirected graph by passing the DFS parent into recursion and only flagging a cycle when a visited neighbor is NOT the immediate parent, since undirected edges naturally back-reference the parent.
task: Cycle detection in undirected graph via parent-tracking DFS

lesson: Detect a cycle in a directed graph with 3-color DFS (white/gray/black): a back-edge to a GRAY (on the current recursion stack) node means a cycle, but an edge to a BLACK node is a safe cross/forward edge, not a cycle.
task: Cycle detection in directed graph via white/gray/black DFS

lesson: Implement topological sort with Kahn's algorithm (BFS on zero-in-degree nodes via collections.deque) so cycle detection is free: if len(output_order) != n after the queue drains, the graph has a cycle and no valid ordering exists.
task: Topological sort with built-in cycle detection via Kahn's algorithm

lesson: Check bipartiteness with a weighted Union-Find carrying an XOR parity bit per node (relative to its parent): union two endpoints as 'must differ', and if find() shows they already share a root with equal parity, the edge closes an odd cycle -> not bipartite.
task: Bipartite check via weighted (parity) union-find, cross-validated against BFS 2-coloring

lesson: Implement Tarjan's SCC iteratively by pushing (node, iter(neighbors)) frames onto an explicit work_stack and resuming the same iterator object on re-entry, so deep graphs (thousands of nodes) never hit Python's recursion limit like a recursive Tarjan's would.
task: Iterative Tarjan's strongly-connected-components on a graph too deep for recursive DFS
